one_line_takeaway: LLMs encode hallucination/truthfulness signals through two mechanistically distinct and causally verified pathways -- one anchored to question-answer attention flow, one self-contained in the generated answer -- and exploiting this split (via gated mixture-of-probes and attention reweighting) measurably improves hallucination-detection AUC over prior probing and uncertainty baselines.
key_finding: Across all 12 models and 4 datasets, truthfulness encoding bifurcates cleanly into Q-Anchored and A-Anchored modes rather than forming a single unified mechanism, and this split is causally validated (not just correlational) via knockout/patching/ablation interventions. Q-Anchored encoding is tied to the model's knowledge boundary (higher answer accuracy, more popular/common entities) while A-Anchored encoding handles long-tail knowledge the model lacks. Internal representations also encode which pathway is active (pathway-classification AUCs ranging from 75.10 to 92.66 across Llama-3-8B, Llama-3-70B, and Mistral-7B-v0.3 on PopQA/TriviaQA/HotpotQA/NQ). Leveraging this, the proposed MoP and PR detection methods outperform baselines on hallucination-detection AUC: on Llama-3-8B, PR reaches 94.01 (PopQA), 83.13 (TriviaQA), 87.81 (HotpotQA), 79.10 (NQ); on Mistral-7B-v0.3, PR reaches 93.09/84.36/89.03/79.09 on the same four datasets respectively -- consistently ahead of the strongest baseline, a standard Probing Baseline (Llama-3-8B: 88.71/77.58/82.23/70.20; Mistral-7B-v0.3: 87.39/81.74/83.19/73.60), and far ahead of P(True), Logits-mean, and Logits-min baselines. Ablations (MoP-RandomGate, MoP-VanillaExperts) confirm both pathway specialization and the self-awareness gating mechanism are necessary, not just an ensembling effect.
core_contribution: The paper identifies two distinct internal information pathways by which LLMs encode truthfulness/hallucination signals in their hidden states: a 'Question-Anchored' (Q-Anchored) pathway that depends on information flowing from the input question to the generated answer, and an 'Answer-Anchored' (A-Anchored) pathway that derives self-contained evidence from the generated answer alone, largely independent of the question. The authors validate this bimodal mechanism via saliency analysis, attention knockout, and token patching across 12 LLMs (base, instruction-tuned, and reasoning-oriented, spanning Llama-3.2-1B/3B, Llama-3-8B/70B, Mistral-7B-v0.1/v0.3 base and instruct, Qwen3-8B/32B) and 4 QA datasets. They further show the two pathways correlate with the model's knowledge boundary (Q-Anchored dominates for well-known/high-popularity entities, A-Anchored for long-tail facts) and that LLM internal states can themselves distinguish which pathway is active ('self-awareness' of pathway identity). Building on these mechanistic findings, they propose two hallucination-detection methods: Mixture-of-Probes (MoP), which combines two pathway-specialized expert probes via a self-awareness gating network, and Pathway Reweighting (PR), a plug-and-play method that rescales attention from answer to question tokens based on the predicted pathway to amplify pathway-relevant cues, applied only at detection time (not during generation).
threat_model: None. This is an interpretability/mechanistic-analysis paper about hallucination detection, not an adversarial or security paper. There is no attacker, no adversarial perturbation, no threat model, and no security property being defended. Framing it in security terms would be forcing a frame the paper does not use.
limitations: The authors' own stated limitation: the method assumes white-box access to internal model representations (hidden states/attention), which is unavailable in strictly black-box deployment settings; they explicitly leave black-box approximations to future work. Additional limitations evident from the text (not stated as such by authors but implicit in scope): all experiments are confined to short-form factoid QA (PopQA, TriviaQA, HotpotQA, Natural Questions) rather than long-form or open-ended generation; the analysis relies on a specific notion of 'exact question/answer tokens' derived from semantic frame theory, and robustness to alternative token selections is only partially explored (deferred to Appendix B.2); PR and MoP add detection-time-only parameters/adapters, so the practical overhead and generalization outside the tested model/dataset combinations is not characterized in the main text.

quantitative_results: Pathway classification AUC (Table 2): Llama-3-8B 87.80/75.10/86.31/78.31 on PopQA/TriviaQA/HotpotQA/NQ; Llama-3-70B 92.66/83.91/87.34/84.14; Mistral-7B-v0.3 87.64/85.87/92.13/84.83

Hallucination detection AUC, Llama-3-8B (Table 3): P(True) 55.85/49.92/52.14/53.27; Logits-mean 74.52/60.39/51.94/52.63; Logits-min 85.36/70.89/61.28/56.50; Probing Baseline 88.71/77.58/82.23/70.20; MoP-RandomGate 75.52/69.17/79.88/66.56; MoP-VanillaExperts 89.11/78.73/84.57/71.21; MoP 92.11/81.18/85.45/74.64; PR 94.01/83.13/87.81/79.10 (all on PopQA/TriviaQA/HotpotQA/NQ respectively)

Hallucination detection AUC, Mistral-7B-v0.3 (Table 3): P(True) 45.49/47.61/57.87/52.79; Logits-mean 69.52/66.76/55.45/57.88; Logits-min 87.05/77.33/68.08/54.40; Probing Baseline 87.39/81.74/83.19/73.60; MoP-RandomGate 79.81/70.88/72.23/61.19; MoP-VanillaExperts 88.53/80.93/82.93/73.77; MoP 91.66/83.57/85.82/76.87; PR 93.09/84.36/89.03/79.09 (all on PopQA/TriviaQA/HotpotQA/NQ respectively)
Abstract/intro claim: proposed pathway-aware methods achieve up to a 10% AUC gain over competing approaches across datasets and models
baselines_compared: Probing Baseline (linear probe on hidden activations, e.g. Chen et al. 2024; Orgad et al. 2025)
MoP-RandomGate (ablation: random routing instead of self-awareness gate)
MoP-VanillaExperts (ablation: two vanilla probes instead of pathway-specialized experts)
